narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 42% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your low rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 at school—both in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's mid-range position—along with a low LSAT score of 22, well below the average of around 37. You also come from a low family income bracket, identify as Asian in a dataset where most students are White, have a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.7 that's above average, and plan to attend full-time like nearly all applicants, while being female in a slightly male-dominated group. The factors that most strongly influenced this prediction, in order of their impact, were your Year 3 school decile, your LSAT score, your Year 1 school decile, your family income, your racial background, your undergraduate GPA, your gender, and your full-time status. Each of these pushed the model toward predicting a higher risk of bar failure, even your above-average GPA, which surprisingly didn't outweigh the others in this case. For instance, your low Year 3 decile had the single biggest pull toward that unfavorable outcome, followed closely by your LSAT, showing how early academic struggles and test performance weighed heavily here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your low deciles and LSAT stand out as bigger drags than your strengths like GPA. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to boost that score toward the average, seek scholarships or support to offset income challenges, and build a stronger academic record in any ongoing studies to lift those decile rankings. With dedication to these areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor and strengthen your overall profile for bar success. Keep pushing—you have a solid GPA foundation to build on.
